nodes.name: Platform Operator
nodes.description: Human operator who authors ScheduledMachine custom resources and manages the controller.

nodes.name: Management Kubernetes Cluster
nodes.description: Kubernetes cluster (>=1.27) that hosts the 5-Spot controller alongside Cluster API controllers. Acts as the control plane for time-based machine scheduling.

nodes.name: CAPI Workload Cluster
nodes.description: Target CAPI-managed workload cluster (e.g. k0s via k0smotron) that physical nodes join when their schedule is active.

nodes.name: 5-Spot Controller
nodes.description: Rust kube-rs controller that reconciles ScheduledMachine CRs, evaluates schedules every ~60s, and drives the machine lifecycle (Pending -> Scheduled -> Active -> Removing -> Inactive).

least-privilege.description: Controller ServiceAccount is bound to a ClusterRole scoped to CRD, CAPI, bootstrap/infra providers, events, secrets (read), nodes and pod eviction. No cluster-admin.

container-hardening.description: Non-root UID 65534, read-only root filesystem, all Linux capabilities dropped, no privilege escalation, distroless base image.

leader-election-ha.description: Leader election via Kubernetes Lease (coordination.k8s.io) with 15s lease, 10s renew deadline, 2s retry; non-leader replicas stand by for ~15s failover.

secret-scanning.description: Gitleaks pre-commit hook and CI scan prevent credentials from being committed to the repository.

nodes.name: ScheduledMachine Custom Resource
nodes.description: Namespaced CRD (5spot.finos.org/v1beta1) declaring a machine, its schedule as a REQUIRED reference to a spot-schedule provider object (spec.schedule: apiVersion/kind/name in the spotschedules.5spot.finos.org group, same namespace — ADR 0009), a top-level spec.enabled administrative master switch, inline bootstrap/infrastructure specs, priority, grace periods, and kill switch.

nodes.name: Kubernetes API Server
nodes.description: Management cluster API server. Stores ScheduledMachine CRs, Leases, Events, Secrets, and the CAPI Machine/bootstrap/infrastructure resources that 5-Spot creates.

nodes.name: Cluster API Core Controller
nodes.description: Upstream CAPI controller that reconciles Machine objects created by 5-Spot and orchestrates bootstrap and infrastructure provider handoff.

nodes.name: CAPI Bootstrap Provider
nodes.description: Bootstrap provider (default example: k0smotron K0sWorkerConfig) that produces cloud-init / bootstrap data for joining the workload cluster. 5-Spot is provider-agnostic via bootstrap.cluster.x-k8s.io wildcards.

nodes.name: CAPI Infrastructure Provider
nodes.description: Infrastructure provider (default example: k0smotron RemoteMachine) that provisions the underlying compute. For RemoteMachine, this means SSHing to a pre-existing physical node and installing k0s.

nodes.name: Physical Target Node
nodes.description: Bare-metal or VM host declared in ScheduledMachine.spec.infrastructureSpec (address/port/user). Reached by the CAPI infrastructure provider over SSH.

nodes.name: Prometheus
nodes.description: Monitoring-namespace Prometheus scraping controller metrics (five_spot_up and reconciliation metrics) every scrape interval.

nodes.name: Kubelet
nodes.description: Node-local kubelet performing HTTP liveness (/healthz) and readiness (/readyz) probes against the controller.

nodes.name: Workload Cluster Kubernetes API Server
nodes.description: API server of the CAPI-managed workload cluster (e.g. k0smotron-hosted k0s). Distinct trust boundary from the management cluster API: accepts its own tokens, its own RBAC, and owns the Node objects plus the 5spot-system namespace that backs emergency reclaim.

agent-pod-security-boundary.description: Deny-by-default ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (5spot-agent-pod-security) scoped to pods in 5spot-system. Because Kubernetes admission is conjunctive (no policy can override another engine's deny), the cluster's pod-security baseline (PSA/Gatekeeper/Kyverno) must exempt the namespace for the two privileged 5-Spot node agents to land; this policy is the paired compensating guardrail behind that exemption. It allowlists exactly the two agent ServiceAccounts' documented posture — kata-config-agent: privileged + hostPID + hostPath '/'; reclaim-agent: hostPID + NET_ADMIN + hostPath '/proc','/etc/machine-id' — denies hostNetwork/hostIPC outright, denies every risky attribute for any other identity in the namespace, and makes the compensating controls (readOnlyRootFilesystem on privileged containers, seccomp RuntimeDefault) mandatory. failurePolicy=Fail (fails closed). See ADR 0004.

nodes.name: 5-Spot Reclaim Agent
nodes.description: Node-side DaemonSet on the workload cluster that watches /proc for processes matching ScheduledMachine.spec.killIfCommands and, on match, writes reclaim annotations onto its own Node object. Runs in-pod with hostPID=true and host /proc mounted read-only; consumes its per-node ConfigMap via a kube API watcher scoped by field selector metadata.name=reclaim-agent-<NODE_NAME> in namespace 5spot-system. Distinct field manager (5spot-reclaim-agent) attributes every write.

least-privilege.description: Agent holds a namespaced Role in 5spot-system granting get,list,watch on ConfigMaps only, plus the kubelet node-scoped token for patching its own Node's annotations. No cluster-wide writes.

container-hardening.description: Explicit pod-level securityContext, read-only root filesystem, host /proc mounted read-only. Architectural-necessity Trivy suppressions (KSV-0010/0012/0020/0021/0023/0105/0118/0121) are documented with rationale in .trivyignore.

nodes.name: Reclaim Request Annotation
nodes.description: Ephemeral annotation triple on a workload Node written by the reclaim agent and read by the 5-Spot controller: 5spot.finos.org/reclaim-requested="true", /reclaim-reason, /reclaim-requested-at. Cleared by the controller as step 6 of the 7-step emergency-reclaim ordering contract.

nodes.name: Per-Node Reclaim Agent ConfigMap
nodes.description: Per-node ConfigMap named reclaim-agent-<node-name> in namespace 5spot-system on the workload cluster. Controller-projected from ScheduledMachine.spec.killIfCommands via server-side apply with field manager 5spot-controller-reclaim-agent; consumed by the agent via a kube runtime watcher scoped by field selector. Empty killIfCommands deletes the ConfigMap; 404-on-delete is benign by contract.

nodes.name: 5-Spot Kata Config Agent
nodes.description: Privileged node-side DaemonSet on the workload cluster that delivers a per-node Kata containerd drop-in to the host filesystem and bounces the host k0s service so containerd reloads it. Reads its source ConfigMap/Secret from the workload API (keyed by the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config-ref Node annotation set by the controller per ADR 0002), atomically writes the fixed drop-in path /etc/k0s/containerd.d/kata.toml (KATA_CONFIG_DEST_PATH, ADR 0005 — no path travels through the CRD or annotations) under a hostPath mount narrowed to /etc/k0s, then runs `nsenter -t 1 -m -u -i -n -p -- systemctl restart <restartService>` to restart the host k0s service. Runs privileged with hostPID=true. Records the applied content hash (bare SHA-256, or 'absent') in the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config-applied annotation on its own Node before restarting; the hash is the restart-loop guard. Distinct field manager (5spot-kata-config-agent) attributes every write. Lands only on nodes carrying the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config=enabled opt-in label. Exposes fivespot_kata_config_* delivery metrics (writes, deletes, drift corrections, restarts, errors, last-sync staleness) on its HTTP /metrics interface, scraped via prometheus.io pod annotations. See ADR 0003.

least-privilege.description: Agent holds a namespaced Role in 5spot-system granting get-only on ConfigMaps and Secrets (read-only source resolution by exact name — no list/watch, KSV-0113), plus a ClusterRole granting get,patch on Nodes for its OWN Node only (read the kata-config-ref annotation; record kata-config-applied; remove the opt-in label on tear-down). No ConfigMap/Secret writes; no Job RBAC.

container-hardening.description: privileged=true and hostPID=true are unavoidable: setns() into host namespaces and nsenter -t 1 into host PID 1 (systemd) require them to restart the host k0s service. Compensating controls: opt-in only (lands solely on label-gated nodes), readOnlyRootFilesystem=true on the container, seccompProfile RuntimeDefault, and a node-scoped RBAC footprint. The privileged posture and its Trivy suppressions are documented with architectural rationale in .trivyignore and docs/src/security/threat-model.md.

host-path-containment.description: No host path travels through the contract (ADR 0005): spec.kata has no destPath field, the kata-config-ref annotation carries no path, and the kata-config-applied annotation is a bare content hash — so neither a CR author nor a nodes-patch holder can steer where the privileged agent writes or unlinks. The destination is the compile-time constant /etc/k0s/containerd.d/kata.toml; every write AND tear-down unlink still resolves through confine_dest_path (/etc/k0s/ prefix + .toml suffix, lexical ../. rejection, canonicalized parent must remain under the canonicalized base — defeats host-side symlinked-directory games) as defense-in-depth, fail closed. The hostPath mount is narrowed to /etc/k0s.

nodes.name: Per-Node Kata Config ConfigMap
nodes.description: Source ConfigMap or Secret on the workload cluster, in spec.kata.namespace (default 5spot-system), holding the Kata containerd drop-in. Platform-/Flux-managed — 5-Spot never creates, writes, or deletes it. The controller resolves it read-only (existence check) and the kata config agent GETs it by exact name each poll tick, keyed by the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config-ref Node annotation. If absent, delivery fails fast with a status reason and the Node is not opted in (ADR 0002).

nodes.name: Spot Schedule Provider
nodes.description: Controller implementing the spotschedules.5spot.finos.org provider contract (ADR 0006, ADR 0009). Reconciles a provider CRD and publishes a single duck-typed status.active boolean plus a Ready condition. 5-Spot ships two FIRST-PARTY providers as standalone controllers: TimeBasedSpotSchedule (the CORE/DEFAULT provider — day/hour/timezone windows, the reified former inline spec.schedule) and CapitalMarketsSchedule (exchange sessions, statutory holidays, early closes); third parties may ship their own. Every ScheduledMachine references exactly one provider via spec.schedule (ADR 0009). 5-Spot treats the provider as an UNTRUSTED INPUT regardless of who ships it: it can start/stop the ScheduledMachines that reference it (same blast radius as editing the referenced provider object), bounded to same-namespace SMs that named it. 5-Spot never reads the provider spec and never writes the provider object.

least-privilege.description: A reference provider's own controller holds update,patch on its OWN kind's /status subresource only (a separate Role, not 5-Spot's ClusterRole). It needs no access to ScheduledMachine or 5-Spot resources.

nodes.name: Spot Schedule Provider Custom Resource
nodes.description: Namespaced CRD in the spotschedules.5spot.finos.org group (TimeBasedSpotSchedule, CapitalMarketsSchedule, or a third-party kind), stored in the management cluster API. Owned by its provider controller, which writes status.active (the authoritative active/inactive signal), a Ready condition, observedGeneration, and lastTransitionTime. Referenced by ScheduledMachine.spec.schedule (apiVersion/kind/name, same namespace) — the single, required activation source after ADR 0009.

relationships.description: The management cluster hosts the 5-Spot controller, the Kubernetes API, the CAPI provider controllers, and any external spot-schedule provider controllers (ADR 0006).

When a schedule is active, the physical node becomes a worker in the CAPI-managed workload cluster. The workload cluster also hosts its own API server and, where ScheduledMachine.spec.killIfCommands is set, the 5-Spot reclaim agent as a DaemonSet; where ScheduledMachine.spec.kata is set, the 5-Spot kata config agent as a DaemonSet.
The platform operator authors and applies ScheduledMachine custom resources.
Operator applies manifests via kubectl to the Kubernetes API.
Controller watches ScheduledMachine CRs and creates CAPI Machine, bootstrap, and infrastructure resources via the Kubernetes API. Also renews the leader-election Lease.

boundary-protection-egress.description: Controller NetworkPolicy restricts egress to port 6443 (kube-apiserver) and DNS only; all other egress denied to prevent exfiltration.

relationships.description: CAPI core reads Machine objects created by 5-Spot from the API server and drives bootstrap + infrastructure reconciliation.
Infrastructure provider (e.g. k0smotron RemoteMachine) SSHes to the declared address to install k0s and join the workload cluster.
Prometheus scrapes /metrics on port 8080; allowed only from the monitoring namespace by NetworkPolicy.

boundary-protection-ingress.description: Only monitoring namespace may reach :8080 per NetworkPolicy ingress rules.

relationships.description: Kubelet probes /healthz and /readyz on port 8081 for liveness and readiness.
ScheduledMachine custom resources are persisted by the Kubernetes API server in etcd.

Controller reaches into the workload cluster API to project the per-node reclaim-agent ConfigMap (server-side apply), stamp the 5spot.finos.org/reclaim-agent=enabled label onto backing Nodes, watch Node updates for the reclaim-requested annotation, issue best-effort drains, and clear the reclaim annotations as step 6 of the emergency-reclaim contract.

audit-attribution.description: All controller writes to the workload cluster API use the distinct field manager '5spot-controller-reclaim-agent' so audit logs attribute projection writes separately from the main reconciler. Supports non-repudiation in regulated-banking review.

relationships.description: Reclaim agent uses the workload cluster API for two scoped operations: a field-selector-scoped watch on its own per-node ConfigMap in 5spot-system, and a merge-patch on its own Node's annotations via the kubelet node-scoped token on process match.

node-scoped-credentials.description: Agent writes to Node objects only via the kubelet node-scoped token (patch nodes/<self>). ConfigMap reads are gated by a namespaced Role/RoleBinding in 5spot-system granting get,list,watch only. No cluster-wide API access.

relationships.description: The per-node reclaim-agent ConfigMap is persisted in the workload cluster API server's etcd, namespace 5spot-system.
The reclaim-request annotation triple lives on the physical Node's metadata and is the agent→controller signalling surface for emergency reclaim.

Controller reads (get) the spec.kata source object on the workload cluster (read-only) and, if present, stamps the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config=enabled opt-in label plus the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config-ref annotation onto the backing Node. If the object or its namespace is absent it clears the opt-in and reports a fail-fast status condition. 5-Spot writes no ConfigMap/Secret and creates no namespace.

audit-attribution.description: Kata delivery performs no object writes: the controller reads the source object on the workload cluster (read-only) and patches only the Node (opt-in label + reference annotation). The agent's read uses its workload-cluster ServiceAccount scoped to spec.kata.namespace (default 5spot-system). See ADR 0002.

relationships.description: Kata config agent uses the workload cluster API for: a read-only get/watch of the source ConfigMap/Secret named by its Node's 5spot.finos.org/kata-config-ref annotation, and merge-patches on its OWN Node — recording the applied content hash in the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config-applied annotation on write (before the host restart), and (on tear-down, when the controller has cleared the ref annotation) unlinking the fixed drop-in path then removing the opt-in label to deschedule itself. Scoped to its own Node by name.

node-scoped-credentials.description: Agent writes to Node objects only via the kubelet node-scoped token (patch nodes/<self>). ConfigMap reads are gated by a namespaced Role/RoleBinding in 5spot-system granting get,list,watch only. No cluster-wide API access.

relationships.description: Kata config agent writes the projected drop-in to the fixed host path /etc/k0s/containerd.d/kata.toml (atomic temp-file + rename under a hostPath mount narrowed to /etc/k0s, ADR 0005) and restarts the host k0s service via `nsenter -t 1 -m -u -i -n -p -- systemctl restart <restartService>`, which bounces containerd so it reloads the drop-in. This is the only host-filesystem write and host command execution in 5-Spot.

host-isolation-boundary.description: Host write + restart require privileged=true and hostPID=true on the agent pod (setns into host namespaces; nsenter to host PID 1 systemd). The escalation is bounded by opt-in label-gating (the agent lands only on nodes the controller has labelled, which only happens when an SM with kata binds that node), readOnlyRootFilesystem on the container, and atomic, idempotent, hash-gated writes that restart at most once per distinct config change. Documented in .trivyignore and docs/src/security/threat-model.md.

relationships.description: The per-node kata-config ConfigMap is persisted in the workload cluster API server's etcd, namespace 5spot-system.
Spot-schedule provider custom resources (spotschedules.5spot.finos.org) are persisted by the management cluster API server in etcd, in the referencing ScheduledMachine's namespace.

The external provider controller reconciles its own CRD and PATCHes status.active (plus a Ready condition, observedGeneration, lastTransitionTime) onto the spot-schedule custom resource via the management cluster API. It writes only its own kind's /status subresource.

Controller watches the spot-schedule provider custom resource named by ScheduledMachine.spec.schedule (the single required activation source, ADR 0009) and reads its duck-typed status.active (and Ready condition). Event-driven via a dynamic per-GVK watch (Api<DynamicObject> resolved through kube discovery) mapped back to referencing ScheduledMachines through an in-memory reverse index; provider status.active transitions trigger SM reconciles at watch latency. Read-only: 5-Spot never writes the provider object. ADR 0006 §5.

least-privilege-readonly-group-pinned.description: Controller RBAC grants get,list,watch ONLY on spotschedules.5spot.finos.org/* — no write to any provider resource. A CEL XValidation on spec.schedule.apiVersion pins the group to exactly spotschedules.5spot.finos.org (any served version accepted, ADR 0007), rejecting references to any other group at admission. References are same-namespace only. Providers are untrusted inputs whose blast radius (flapping the SMs that reference them) equals editing the referenced provider object; mitigations are audited via the SpotScheduleResolved condition and transition-rate metrics.

flows.name: Schedule Activation (machine enters window)
flows.description: When the current time enters a ScheduledMachine's active window, 5-Spot creates the bootstrap + infrastructure resources and a CAPI Machine so that CAPI provisions the physical node and joins it to the workload cluster.

transitions.description: Operator applies a ScheduledMachine CR declaring schedule, bootstrap spec, infrastructure spec, and cluster name.

Controller watch observes the CR and the provider object named by spec.schedule; the provider's status.active (e.g. TimeBasedSpotSchedule evaluating daysOfWeek/hoursOfDay in its timezone) reports the machine should be active (ADR 0009).
Controller creates K0sWorkerConfig (bootstrap), RemoteMachine (infra), and a CAPI Machine referencing both; phase -> Scheduled.
CAPI core reconciles the Machine and hands off to bootstrap + infra providers.
Infrastructure provider SSHes to the physical node, installs k0s with bootstrap data, and joins the workload cluster; phase -> Active.

graceful-lifecycle.description: Controller records Kubernetes Events at every phase transition for auditability.

flows.name: Schedule Deactivation (machine exits window or kill switch)
flows.description: When the window closes (or killSwitch is set), the controller cordons the node, evicts pods within nodeDrainTimeout, then deletes the CAPI Machine so the infra provider tears it down.

transitions.description: Scheduler tick detects out-of-window (or killSwitch=true); phase -> Removing; gracefulShutdownTimeout starts.
Controller cordons the node (patch spec.unschedulable=true) and creates pod eviction requests, respecting nodeDrainTimeout.
Controller deletes the CAPI Machine (and associated bootstrap/infra objects if owned); phase -> Inactive / UnScheduled.
CAPI core propagates deletion to providers; physical node is removed from the workload cluster.

graceful-shutdown.description: Drain respects configured gracefulShutdownTimeout and nodeDrainTimeout so that workloads are evicted cleanly before node removal.

flows.name: Emergency Reclaim (process-match eject)
flows.description: A matched process on a workload node triggers an ASAP eject that skips graceful drain. The 7-step ordering contract is load-bearing: spec.enabled=false (step 5, ADR 0009) must be written BEFORE the annotation clear (step 6) so a crash between them is replay-safe. Full narrative in docs/src/concepts/emergency-reclaim.md.

transitions.description: Agent detects a process matching spec.killIfCommands via /proc scan and PATCHes the reclaim annotation triple (5spot.finos.org/reclaim-requested="true", /reclaim-reason, /reclaim-requested-at) onto its own Node via the kubelet node-scoped token. Field manager: 5spot-reclaim-agent.
Controller Node watch fires. check_emergency_reclaim reads the annotation, transitions status.phase = EmergencyRemove, and emits Event Reason: EmergencyReclaim with the annotation's reason string.

Best-effort kubectl drain --grace-period=0 --force --disable-eviction against the workload cluster, bounded by EMERGENCY_DRAIN_TIMEOUT_SECS=60. Failure is log-and-continue — the eject has already been committed to.
Controller deletes the CAPI Machine on the management cluster — no PDB respect, no graceful shutdown. CAPI propagates deletion to providers and the physical node leaves the workload cluster.

Controller PATCHes ScheduledMachine spec.enabled=false (the loop-breaker: without this, the provider's next active window re-adds the node and the agent re-fires forever) and emits Event Reason: EmergencyReclaimDisabledSchedule. spec.enabled is SM-scoped (ADR 0009) — the provider object is never patched, since it may be shared by other SMs. Load-bearing: must run BEFORE step 6.

Controller PATCHes the Node to null all three reclaim annotations. Best-effort: a crash between steps 5 and 6 is replay-safe because the agent re-annotates on the next poll cycle if the matched process is still running.
Controller transitions ScheduledMachine status.phase = Disabled. Re-enable is a manual operator action (kubectl patch ... spec.enabled=true).

ordering-idempotence.description: The load-bearing ordering of steps 5 (disable-schedule PATCH) before 6 (annotation-clear PATCH) guarantees crash-recovery idempotence. A crash after step 6 but before step 7 is caught by the PHASE_EMERGENCY_REMOVE match arm in reconcile(), which finishes the transition to Disabled on the next reconcile.

flows.name: Kata Config Delivery (per-node drop-in + k0s restart)
flows.description: A ScheduledMachine with spec.kata set drives delivery of a Kata containerd drop-in onto its backing node and a one-shot host k0s restart so containerd reloads it. The applied-hash annotation (step 4, written BEFORE the restart in step 5) is the load-bearing restart-loop guard: it ensures the agent restarts at most once per distinct config change even though the restart SIGKILLs the agent. Decisions: ADR 0002 (contract + projection) and ADR 0003 (host write + nsenter restart).

transitions.description: Controller (Active phase, best-effort, workload-cluster-scoped) reads (get) the source Secret/ConfigMap named in spec.kata in spec.kata.namespace (default 5spot-system) on the workload cluster. If present, it stamps 5spot.finos.org/kata-config=enabled plus the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config-ref annotation on the backing Node; if absent, it clears the opt-in and reports a fail-fast status reason (SourceNotFound / TargetNamespaceMissing).
The kata config agent DaemonSet (scheduled onto the node by the opt-in label) watches its per-node ConfigMap and reads the projected drop-in content at /etc/5spot/kata-config/<key>.

Agent compares the SHA-256 of the source content against the fixed host drop-in path (/etc/k0s/containerd.d/kata.toml under the /host mount) and the kata-config-applied annotation. On mismatch it atomically writes the drop-in (temp-file + rename, mode 0644, root:root) to that fixed path. If the source key is absent it unlinks the host file (GitOps delete).

Agent records the applied hash (bare content SHA-256, or 'absent' after tear-down) in the 5spot.finos.org/kata-config-applied annotation on its own Node via the kubelet node-scoped token. Load-bearing: written BEFORE the restart so a crash/SIGKILL during restart does not re-trigger on the next loop.

Agent runs `nsenter -t 1 -m -u -i -n -p -- systemctl restart <restartService>` to restart the host k0s service; containerd reloads the new drop-in. The restart SIGKILLs the agent pod; systemd still completes the D-Bus job. Kubelet restarts the agent; on the next loop hashes match the applied annotation, so it is a no-op. Single-cycle convergence.

restart-loop-idempotence.description: The applied-hash annotation written before the restart (step 4) guarantees the agent restarts the host service at most once per distinct config change, despite the restart killing the agent. Drift correction rewrites the file but does not re-restart when the applied hash already matches.

flows.name: Spot Schedule Activation (provider-driven active/inactive)
flows.description: Every ScheduledMachine delegates its active/inactive decision to the single provider named by spec.schedule (ADR 0009) via that provider's duck-typed status.active — the default being the first-party TimeBasedSpotSchedule. The controller watches the provider CR event-driven; the provider verdict IS the should-be-active decision (no inline schedule to AND with after ADR 0009; killSwitch always wins). An unresolved/unready provider never flaps machines: the controller surfaces SpotScheduleResolved=False and holds last-known state (fail-inactive when never resolved). ADR 0006 §4, ADR 0009.

transitions.description: External provider controller evaluates its own spec (e.g. exchange calendar/holidays/early-closes) and PATCHes status.active (and the Ready condition) onto its CR. 5-Spot never reads the provider spec — only this status.

Controller's dynamic watch on the provider GVK fires; the reverse index maps the event to the referencing ScheduledMachine(s) and enqueues them. The resolver reads status.active and the Ready condition defensively (missing CRD/object/status or Ready=False ⇒ Unresolved, surfaced as SpotScheduleResolved=False, not inactive).

Controller composes the provider verdict with evaluate_schedule (AND when spec.schedule is also set; killSwitch overrides). If the composite verdict is active and the machine is not yet up, it proceeds into flow-schedule-activation (create bootstrap/infra + CAPI Machine).

On a provider transition to status.active=false (composite verdict inactive), the controller proceeds into flow-schedule-deactivation (cordon, drain within nodeDrainTimeout, delete the CAPI Machine). Provider Unresolved does NOT trigger this — last-known state is held.

untrusted-provider-failsafe.description: Providers are untrusted inputs. Group-pinned read-only RBAC (get,list,watch on spotschedules.5spot.finos.org/* only), same-namespace-only references, and the CEL group pin bound the blast radius to SMs that named the provider. Unresolved references hold last-known state and fail inactive when never resolved, so a missing/unready/compromised provider degrades to a loud SpotScheduleResolved=False status rather than flapping machines. Transition-rate metrics + an alert detect flapping.

supply-chain-scanning.description: Repository is scanned by Semgrep OSS (SAST), Grype (container image, VEX-aware), Trivy (IaC config), cargo-audit + cargo-deny (RustSec advisories, license allow-list, source restrictions), and Gitleaks (secrets); OpenSSF Scorecard publishes supply-chain posture; SPDX license identifiers on source files.
